content: Two stones begin to fall from rest from the same height, with the second stone starting to fall ' $\Delta \mathrm{t}$ ' seconds after the first falls from rest. The distance of separation between the two stones becomes ' H ', ' $\mathrm{t}_0$ ' seconds after the first stone starts its motion. Then $\mathrm{t}_0$ is equal to
explanation:
To solve this problem, we need to determine the time, denoted as $ \mathrm{t}_0 $, at which the separation between two falling stones becomes $ \mathrm{H} $. The second stone starts falling $ \Delta \mathrm{t} $ seconds after the first stone.

Distance Fallen by the First Stone ($ \mathrm{S}_1 $):

The first stone falls for $ \mathrm{t}_0 $ seconds, so the distance it covers is given by:

$ \mathrm{S}_1 = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{t}_0^2 $

Distance Fallen by the Second Stone ($ \mathrm{S}_2 $):

The second stone starts falling $ \Delta \mathrm{t} $ seconds after the first, so it falls for $ (\mathrm{t}_0 - \Delta \mathrm{t}) $ seconds. Thus, the distance it covers is:

$ \mathrm{~S}_2 = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} (\mathrm{t}_0 - \Delta \mathrm{t})^2 $

Distance of Separation ($ \mathrm{H} $):

The separation distance between the two stones is:

$ \mathrm{H} = \mathrm{S}_1 - \mathrm{S}_2 = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{t}_0^2 - \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} (\mathrm{t}_0 - \Delta \mathrm{t})^2 $

Simplifying the equation:

$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{H} & = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{t}_0^2 - \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} (\mathrm{t}_0^2 - 2 \mathrm{t}_0 \Delta \mathrm{t} + (\Delta \mathrm{t})^2) \\ & = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \cdot (2 \mathrm{t}_0 \Delta \mathrm{t} - (\Delta \mathrm{t})^2) \\ & = \mathrm{g} \cdot (\mathrm{t}_0 \Delta \mathrm{t} - \frac{1}{2} \Delta \mathrm{t}^2) \end{aligned} $

Expression for $ \mathrm{t}_0 $:

Solving the above equation for $ \mathrm{t}_0 $, we have:

$ \mathrm{H} = \mathrm{g} (\mathrm{t}_0 \Delta \mathrm{t} - \frac{1}{2} \Delta \mathrm{t}^2) $

$ \mathrm{t}_0 \Delta \mathrm{t} = \frac{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{g}} + \frac{1}{2} \Delta \mathrm{t}^2 $

$ \mathrm{t}_0 = \frac{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{g} \Delta \mathrm{t}} + \frac{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{2} $

Therefore, the time $ \mathrm{t}_0 $ at which the separation between the two stones becomes $ \mathrm{H} $ is given by:

$ \mathrm{t}_0 = \frac{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{g} \Delta \mathrm{t}} + \frac{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{2} $

content: A body is moving along a straight line with initial velocity $$v_0$$. Its acceleration $$a$$ is constant. After $$t$$ seconds, its velocity becomes $$v$$. The average velocity of the body over the given time interval is
explanation:
To determine the average velocity of a body moving along a straight line with a constant acceleration $a$, given its initial velocity $v_0$ and its final velocity $v$ after $t$ seconds, we can use the following approach:

Information Given:

Initial velocity, $v_0$

Final velocity after time $t$, $v$

Constant acceleration, $a$

Using the Equation of Motion:

The equation related to velocity and displacement is:

$ v^2 = u^2 + 2as $

Rearranging for displacement $s$:

$ s = \frac{v^2 - v_0^2}{2a} $

This equation gives us the total distance covered.

Calculating Average Velocity:

The average velocity $\bar{v}$ over an interval is the total distance divided by the total time:

$ \bar{v} = \frac{\text{Total distance}}{\text{Total time}} = \frac{s}{t} $

Substituting for $s$ from the displacement equation:

$ \bar{v} = \frac{\frac{v^2 - v_0^2}{2a}}{t} $

Simplifying:

$ \bar{v} = \frac{v^2 - v_0^2}{2at} $

Hence, the average velocity is given by:

$ v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{v^2 - v_0^2}{2at} $

This formula provides the average velocity of the body over the given time interval $t$.

content: The displacement $$x$$ (in $$\mathrm{m}$$) of a particle of mass $$m$$ (in $$\mathrm{kg}$$) moving in one dimension under the action of a force, is related to time $$t$$ (in sec) by $$t=\sqrt{x}+3$$. The displacement of the particle when its velocity is zero, will be
explanation:
Displacement of the particle related with time is given as

$$t=\sqrt{x}+3 \Rightarrow \sqrt{x}=t-3$$

Squaring on both sides, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & x=(t-3)^2 \\ \Rightarrow & x=t^2-6 t+9 \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{array}$$

Velocity of the particle,

$$v=\frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(t^2-6 t+9\right)=2 t-6$$

when $$v=0$$

i.e $$2 t-6=0 \Rightarrow t=36$$

i.e velocity of particle is zero at $$t=3 \mathrm{~s}$$.

$$\therefore$$ Displacement of particle at $$t=3 \mathrm{~s}$$,

$$\begin{aligned} x=3^2 & -6 \times 3+9 \quad[\text { from Eq. (i) }] \\ & =0 \end{aligned}$$

content:
For a body moving along a straight line, the following $$v$$-$$t$$ graph is obtained.

According to the graph, the displacement during

options.content: uniform acceleration is greater than that during uniform motion
uniform acceleration is less than that during uniform motion
uniform acceleration is equal to that during uniform motion

explanation:
Since, the area under $$v$$-$$t$$ graph gives the displacement of object.

i.e., $$\quad s=$$ Area of $$v$$-$$t$$ graph

As area under uniform motion > Area under uniform acceleration

$$\therefore$$ Displacement during uniform acceleration is less than that during uniform motion.

content: A particle starts from rest. Its acceleration $$a$$ versus time $$t$$ is shown in the figure. The maximum speed of the particle will be
explanation:
As, we know

Area under $$a$$-$$t$$ graph $$=v-u$$

$$\begin{array}{lll} \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 10=v & (\because u=0) \\ \Rightarrow & v=40 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \end{array}$$

content: At a metro station, a girl walks up a stationary escalator in $$20 \mathrm{~s}$$. If she remains stationary on the escalator, then the escalator take her up in $$30 \mathrm{~s}$$. The time taken by her to walk up on the moving escalator will be
explanation:
To find the time taken by the girl to walk up on the moving escalator, we can use the concept of relative speeds.

Let's define:

$ v_{\text{girl}} $ as the speed of the girl walking.

$ v_{\text{escalator}} $ as the speed of the escalator.

When the girl walks up on the stationary escalator, she takes 20 seconds. This gives us:

$$ d = v_{\text{girl}} \times 20 \, \text{s} $$

When the escalator alone moves and the girl does not walk, the time taken is 30 seconds. Thus:

$$ d = v_{\text{escalator}} \times 30 \, \text{s} $$

When the girl walks up on the moving escalator, both her speed and the escalator's speed contribute to her total speed:

$$ d = (v_{\text{girl}} + v_{\text{escalator}}) \times t $$

Setting the distances equal since they all cover distance $ d $:

From $ v_{\text{girl}} $:

$$ v_{\text{girl}} = \frac{d}{20} $$

From $ v_{\text{escalator}} $:

$$ v_{\text{escalator}} = \frac{d}{30} $$

Combining both in the case of the moving escalator:

$$ d = \left(\frac{d}{20} + \frac{d}{30}\right) \times t $$

Simplifying the combination:

$$ 1 = \left(\frac{1}{20} + \frac{1}{30}\right) \times t $$

Finding a common denominator and solving:

$$ \frac{1}{20} + \frac{1}{30} = \frac{3}{60} + \frac{2}{60} = \frac{5}{60} = \frac{1}{12} $$

Now, solve for $ t $:

$$ 1 = \frac{1}{12} \times t $$

So:

$$ t = 12 \, \text{s} $$

Hence, the time taken by her to walk up on the moving escalator is 12 seconds.

Option C: 12 s is the correct answer.

content:
The given graph shows the variation of velocity $$v$$ with position $$x$$ for a particle moving along a straight line

Which of the following graph shows the variation of acceleration a with position x?
explanation:
According to velocity-position graph.

$$v=-k x+b\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

where, $$k$$ and $$b$$ are constants differentiating Eq (i) w.r.t $$t$$, we have

$$\begin{gathered} \frac{d v}{d t}=-k \frac{d x}{d t}+0 \\ a=-k v \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{gathered}$$

$$\left[\because\right.$$ acceleration, $$\left.a=\frac{d v}{d t}\right]$$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii),

$$\begin{aligned} & a=-k(-k x+b) \\ & a=k^2 x-k b \quad \text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Correct graph of equation (iii) is represented in option (c).

content: A particle shows distance-time curve as shown in the figure. The maximum instantaneous velocity of the particle is around the point.
explanation:
Instantaneous velocity is given by,

$$ V=\lim \limits_{\Delta t \rightarrow 0} \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}=\frac{d x}{d t} $$

which is the slope of $x$ - $t$ graph.

Here, $\frac{d x}{d t}$ is maximum for the region $Q R$ and minimum for the region $R S$.

$\therefore$ Instantaneous velocity is maximum around $Q$.

content: A car moving with a velocity of $20 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ stopped at a distance of 40 m . If the same car is travelling at double the velocity, the distance travelled by it for same retardation is
explanation:
Given Initial Conditions:

Initial velocity, $ u = 20 \, \mathrm{m/s} $

Stopping distance, $ s = 40 \, \mathrm{m} $

Determine Retardation (Deceleration):

We use the equation from Newton's second law of motion:

$ v^2 = u^2 + 2as $

Since the car stops, its final velocity $ v = 0 $. Therefore, we have:

$ 0^2 - u^2 = 2 \times a \times s $

Substituting the given values:

$ 0 - 20^2 = 2 \times a \times 40 $

$ -400 = 80a $

Solving for $ a $:

$ a = \frac{-400}{80} = -5 \, \mathrm{m/s^2} $

Increased Velocity Condition:

When the velocity is doubled, the new initial velocity is $ u' = 2u = 40 \, \mathrm{m/s} $.

Calculate New Stopping Distance:

Using the same formula with the new initial velocity:

$ 0^2 - (40)^2 = 2 \times (-5) \times s $

$ -1600 = -10s $

Solving for $ s $:

$ s = \frac{1600}{10} = 160 \, \mathrm{m} $

Thus, the stopping distance when the car's velocity is doubled is $ 160 \, \mathrm{m} $.
